title: Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index
subtitle: Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by [V-Dem](#dod:v-dem), based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).
caption:
Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index

Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by V-Dem, based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the share of the population living in poverty and the Electoral Democracy Index for various countries in 2025. The vertical axis represents the share of the population in poverty, defined as living on less than $10 a day, ranging from 0% to over 80%. The horizontal axis shows the Electoral Democracy Index, which ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates the most democratic. Data points are color-coded by continent: North America (orange), South America (brown), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). The data is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices and relates to income or consumption per capita. A general negative trend is visible, where countries with higher democracy scores tend to have lower poverty rates. African nations such as Burundi, Ethiopia, Benin, and Kenya cluster in the upper-left quadrant, showing high poverty shares between 60% and 90% alongside lower democracy indices below 0.5. In contrast, countries like Australia, Chile, and Costa Rica appear in the bottom-right, with poverty shares below 20% and democracy indices above 0.7. European countries generally occupy the lower-right area with high democracy scores and low poverty, while Asian countries like China, Vietnam, and Thailand show mid-range poverty levels with varying democracy scores. South American nations like Peru, Colombia, and Brazil sit in the middle-to-lower right, with moderate poverty and relatively high democracy indices.
